trajectory_summary: The call books, but on the customer's momentum, not the rep's. Angeline arrives as a self-qualified, high-intent buyer who cancelled a competitor contract and is actively seeking Ion — Ella's job was largely to not lose her. The appointment is real and the customer is warm, but Ella left three compounding leverage points untouched: the autonomy theme that recurs three times, the battery backup vulnerability disclosure, and the unprompted brand endorsement that deserved a stronger anchor. The handoff is structurally sound but thin on context the closer needs.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a booked appointment for Friday at 2:30. The booking is genuine — Angeline is warm, credit-qualified, has a new roof, and has prior Ion contact. However, the appointment carries a conditional flag: Angeline explicitly states she will run any contract through her AI. The closer must be briefed on this or the sit is at risk of a repeat cancellation.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Autonomy theme — reflect and differentiate, don't pass through
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I am not trying to be told what I can and cannot do on my property for the next twenty five years.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Angeline's autonomy and contract-control concern surfaces three times (01:39, 07:43, 10:21) and is the exact reason she cancelled her last contract. Ella passes it every time. This is not a generic rapport gap — it is the specific trust filter that will determine whether this appointment holds and whether the closer gets a signed deal. The manager should use all three instances in sequence to show the rep the pattern she missed, then coach the one-sentence reflection move that converts a recurring anxiety into a booking anchor.

key_moments.quote: I just had another solar company. I signed a contract with them yesterday, but then I had my AI go over it. And found AI found some things I didn't care for, so I ended up canceling. I am interested in hearing what Ion would have to say for the things that I need and want.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the highest-leverage opening in the call. Angeline is handing Ella a differentiation moment: she cancelled a competitor on contract integrity grounds and is explicitly asking what Ion offers. The strategic move here is to anchor Ion's identity around transparency and customer control — not to launch into verify. Ella responds with 'Alright. Sounds good. Yeah. We can do what we can do for you. So what type of things were you looking for in the solar?' — a generic open-ended question that treats this as a standard inbound rather than a competitive re-entry. The reactive read holds: Ella missed the first-impression differentiation window. The strategic hypothesis — that Ella was deliberately deferring to let the customer talk first — is plausible but unsupported, because she never circles back to differentiate Ion.

key_moments.quote: I need a battery backup system for just my boiler. I have a gas boiler. It has an electric start. It's also my hot water heater. It's a two in one deal.
key_moments.stewart_read: Angeline has a specific, named need: battery backup for her boiler's electric start. Ion carries the Tesla Powerwall as its only battery product. The gap here is NOT that Ella failed to name the Powerwall — naming a specific product is closer-tier content and would be scope creep per setter_scope_discipline. The gap is that Ella never deployed the universal escape hatch: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — battery options, what size makes sense for your setup, all of that.' Instead Ella says 'We can definitely just see what we can do for you' — a vague non-bridge that neither holds setter scope cleanly nor routes the need to the closer with any specificity. The reactive read holds: the absent escape hatch sentence is the failure, not the absent product name.

key_moments.quote: Yes. The power is out even though it's a gas boiler system, because the electric charge is a power in the middle of winter, I'll freeze to that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Angeline is disclosing a real safety vulnerability: without power, her heating system fails in winter and she will freeze. This is a trust gift per the codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure framework — a health/safety disclosure that deserves specific acknowledgment before any product or process response. Ella's response is 'Mhmm' — a listening signal, not an empathetic acknowledgment. The disclosure is then treated purely as a product need (battery backup) rather than as a human moment that, if reflected back, would have deepened trust significantly. The reactive read holds: Ella heard the need but missed the trust gift.

key_moments.quote: I'm exactly here. They're gonna have to throw my whole dead body out of here. The house is almost 34.
key_moments.stewart_read: Angeline is signaling 34-year tenure in her home with zero ambiguity about staying. This is a high-value qualifier: long-tenure homeowners have the highest ROI on solar (more years to recoup investment, no relocation risk) and the strongest emotional attachment to the property. Ella's response is 'Alright. That's perfect. Alright. Then let me see here.' — a procedural acknowledgment that moves immediately to the credit check. The enthusiasm signal is walked past entirely. This was also a natural rapport anchor: a customer who has lived somewhere for 34 years and says 'they're gonna have to throw my whole dead body out' is giving the rep a human moment to meet.
